Side Lunge.targets: Hip Adductors (origin: pubis; insertion: femur) and Gluteus Medius/Maximus.
Side Lunge.biomechanics: Gently shifts weight side-to-side in the frontal plane to lubricate hip joints and safely stretch musculotendinous junctions.
Side Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand with feet significantly wider than shoulder-width. 2. Push hips back, keeping back flat. 3. Bend right knee, shifting weight to the right foot while left leg stays straight. 4. Push off right foot to center, then shift left.
Side Lunge.cue: Imagine sliding side-to-side on a slippery floor.

Side Lunge.targets: Hip Flexors (Iliopsoas, Rectus Femoris) and Hip Extensors (Hamstrings, Gluteus Maximus).
Side Lunge.biomechanics: Dynamically warms the femoral head in the acetabulum (hip socket) and stretches the attaching tendons like a pendulum.
Side Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand sideways to a wall or rack for balance. 2. Keeping the outside leg relatively straight, swing it forward in a controlled arc. 3. Allow gravity to bring the leg back and through into a stretch. 4. Gradually increase ROM.
Side Lunge.cue: Feel the stretch at the top of the forward and backward swing.

Side Lunge.targets: Gluteus Medius (outer ilium to greater trochanter) and Adductor Magnus (pubis to lower femur).
Side Lunge.biomechanics: Frontal-plane unilateral movement that creates a powerful eccentric and concentric contraction in the primary hip stabilizers.
Side Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand with feet together. 2. Take a large lateral step directly to your right side. 3. As the foot plants, hinge hips back and bend the right knee. 4. Drive forcefully off the right heel and midfoot to return to the start.
Side Lunge.cue: Push the floor away with your bent leg to return to center.

Side Lunge.targets: Gluteus Medius and Minimus (ilium to greater trochanter of the femur).
Side Lunge.biomechanics: Provides constant lateral cable resistance to isolate pure hip abduction without relying on momentum.
Side Lunge.how_to: 1. Attach an ankle cuff to a low pulley around your right ankle. 2. Stand with your left side to the machine. 3. Keeping torso upright, use outer hip muscles to pull your right leg directly out to the side. 4. Squeeze at the peak, then slowly lower.
Side Lunge.cue: Squeeze from the outside of your hips.

Side Lunge.targets: Adductor Group (pubic bone/ischium inserting along the inner femur).
Side Lunge.biomechanics: Provides constant medial cable tension to isolate pure hip adduction against resistance.
Side Lunge.how_to: 1. Attach cuff to a low pulley around your right ankle. 2. Stand with right side to the machine. 3. Keeping torso stable, engage inner thigh to pull the right leg across the front of your standing leg. 4. Squeeze powerfully, then return.
Side Lunge.cue: Imagine squeezing a ball between your thighs.

Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Dynamic circumduction of the humerus to lubricate the glenohumeral joint and warm synovial fluid.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Stand tall and extend arms straight out to your sides. 2. Make small, controlled forward circles. 3. Gradually increase the size of the circles. 4. Reverse direction after 30 seconds.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Draw perfect circles with your fingertips.

Incline Chest Press.targets: Lower Trapezius (thoracic vertebrae to scapular spine) and Rotator Cuff.
Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Active shoulder flexion paired with external rotation to secure the scapula against the ribcage.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Lie face down on the floor or an incline bench. 2. Extend arms in a 'Y' shape with thumbs pointing up to the ceiling. 3. Lift arms by pinching shoulder blades together and down. 4. Lower with control.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Initiate the lift by pulling your shoulder blades down and together.

Incline Chest Press.targets: Clavicular head of Pectoralis Major (clavicle to bicipital groove), Anterior Deltoid, and Triceps.
Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Pushing from a 30-degree incline shifts the mechanical tension to bias the upper pectoral fibers over the mid/lower chest.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Set bench to 30 degrees. 2. Kick dumbbells up to shoulder height. 3. Press the weights up and slightly inward over your upper chest. 4. Lower under control until dumbbells are near chest level.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Drive your biceps towards each other at the top.

Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Provides constant upward and inward arc tension that perfectly mirrors the fiber alignment of the upper chest.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Set cables to the lowest position. 2. Grab D-handles and stand slightly forward to create tension. 3. With a slight bend in the elbows, sweep the handles upward and together until they meet at face height. 4. Lower slowly.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Scoop the air up and in.

Incline Chest Press.targets: Anterior Deltoid (lateral clavicle to deltoid tuberosity).
Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Applies constant cable tension specifically isolating shoulder flexion in the sagittal plane.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Set a low cable with a straight bar or rope. 2. Stand facing away from the machine, cable running between your legs. 3. Keeping arms straight, raise the handle to eye level. 4. Lower with control.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Lift the weight using the front of your shoulder, keeping arms straight.

Incline Chest Press.targets: Triceps Brachii Long Head (infraglenoid tubercle of scapula to olecranon of ulna).
Incline Chest Press.biomechanics: Secures the shoulder in flexion to put the long head of the triceps under a deep mechanical stretch during extension.
Incline Chest Press.how_to: 1. Set cable high with a rope attachment. 2. Face away from the machine, hinging forward slightly, hands behind your head. 3. Extend your elbows to press the rope forward and out. 4. Control the eccentric back.
Incline Chest Press.cue: Pin elbows to your head; only move forearms.

Walking Lunge.biomechanics: Unloaded sagittal plane movement to lubricate the knee and hip joints and groove the exact motor pattern for the workout.
Walking Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand tall. 2. Take a controlled step forward. 3. Lower your hips until both knees are bent at a 90-degree angle. 4. Push off the front foot to return to the start.
Walking Lunge.cue: Keep front knee aligned with second toe.

Walking Lunge.targets: Hip Flexors, Core, and Cardiovascular System.
Walking Lunge.biomechanics: Dynamic unloaded lower-body activation to safely increase heart rate and prepare joints for loaded unilateral work.
Walking Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand tall. 2. Run in place, bringing your knees up to at least hip height. 3. Pump your arms in rhythm with your legs. 4. Keep a brisk pace for 30-60 seconds.
Walking Lunge.cue: Drive your knees high and land softly on the balls of your feet.

Walking Lunge.targets: Quadriceps (femur to tibial tuberosity), Gluteus Maximus, and Hamstrings.
Walking Lunge.biomechanics: Dynamic, unilateral sagittal plane extension of the hip and knee requiring significant core and pelvic stability.
Walking Lunge.how_to: 1. Hold dumbbells at your sides. 2. Step forward and drop your back knee toward the floor. 3. Push entirely through the front foot to stand up and immediately step into the next rep with the opposite leg.

Walking Lunge.biomechanics: Being seated forces hip flexion, which pre-stretches the biarticular hamstrings, allowing for superior tension during knee flexion.
Walking Lunge.how_to: 1. Sit in the machine with the pad resting just above your Achilles tendons. 2. Lock the thigh pad down tight. 3. Curl the weight down and back as far as possible. 4. Control the weight on the way up.
Walking Lunge.cue: Squeeze the hamstring to move the weight, controlling the release.

Walking Lunge.targets: Soleus (posterior tibia/fibula inserting into the calcaneus/heel).
Walking Lunge.biomechanics: Sitting with a bent knee puts the larger gastrocnemius in active insufficiency, purely isolating the underlying soleus muscle.
Walking Lunge.how_to: 1. Sit on a bench and place the ball of one foot on a block. 2. Place a heavy dumbbell on that thigh near the knee. 3. Lower the heel to a deep stretch. 4. Press up onto the toes as high as possible.
Walking Lunge.cue: Push through the ball of your foot.

Bent-Over Row.targets: Erector Spinae (spine/sacrum to ribs/vertebrae) and Rectus Abdominis.
Bent-Over Row.biomechanics: Controlled spinal flexion and extension to lubricate intervertebral discs and prime the core.
Bent-Over Row.how_to: 1. Get on hands and knees. 2. Inhale and arch your back, dropping your belly and lifting your head. 3. Exhale and round your spine upward, tucking your chin to your chest.

Bent-Over Row.targets: Rhomboids (thoracic spinous processes to medial scapular border) and Mid-Traps.
Bent-Over Row.biomechanics: Pure, unweighted scapular retraction against gravity to neurologically prime the upper back before heavy pulling.
Bent-Over Row.how_to: 1. Hinge at the hips with a flat back. 2. Let arms hang straight down. 3. Keep arms straight and raise them straight out to your sides to form a 'T'. 4. Squeeze shoulder blades, then lower.
Bent-Over Row.cue: Pinch a coin between your shoulder blades at the top.

Bent-Over Row.targets: Latissimus Dorsi (thoracolumbar fascia to bicipital groove of humerus), Biceps, and Rear Deltoids.
Bent-Over Row.biomechanics: Heavy sagittal/transverse plane pulling requiring intense isometric contraction of the erector spinae and hamstrings to hold the hinge.
Bent-Over Row.how_to: 1. Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor. 2. Let dumbbells hang down. 3. Row the weights up toward your hip crease/ribcage, keeping elbows close to the body. 4. Lower with control.
Bent-Over Row.cue: Pull your elbows into your back pockets.

Bent-Over Row.targets: Biceps Brachii Long Head (supraglenoid tubercle of scapula to radial tuberosity).
Bent-Over Row.biomechanics: Seated on an incline allows the shoulder to hyperextend behind the torso, putting the long head of the biceps under a massive stretch.
Bent-Over Row.how_to: 1. Set an incline bench facing away from a low cable. 2. Sit and grab a D-handle with one hand. 3. Let the arm hang slightly behind your torso. 4. Curl the weight up without moving your upper arm forward.
Bent-Over Row.cue: Keep elbow pinned back to isolate the stretched bicep.

Bent-Over Row.targets: Posterior Deltoid (spine of scapula to deltoid tuberosity).
Bent-Over Row.biomechanics: Constant cable tension applied directly against horizontal shoulder abduction.
Bent-Over Row.how_to: 1. Stand facing a high dual cable setup. 2. Grab the left cable with your right hand and right cable with your left hand (crossing them). 3. Keep arms slightly bent and pull them apart and backward.
Bent-Over Row.cue: Stretch a giant rubber band across your chest.

Curtsy Lunge.biomechanics: Direct lateral hip abduction to actively prime and wake up the pelvic lateral stabilizers before offset/curtsy patterns without loading the spine.
Curtsy Lunge.how_to: 1. Lie on your side with both legs straight and stacked. 2. Keeping your core engaged, lift the top leg straight up without rotating your pelvis backward. 3. Lower with control.
Curtsy Lunge.cue: Raise the leg by squeezing the side of your hip.

Curtsy Lunge.biomechanics: Active hip abduction and external rotation in the transverse/frontal planes to prime the lateral hip.
Curtsy Lunge.how_to: 1. Get on your hands and knees. 2. Keeping your knee bent at 90 degrees, lift one leg straight out to the side. 3. Avoid rotating your lower back or shifting your weight heavily.
Curtsy Lunge.cue: Don't spill the glass of water on your lower back.

Curtsy Lunge.targets: Gluteus Medius, Gluteus Maximus, and Adductors.
Curtsy Lunge.biomechanics: A multi-planar lunge that forces the lateral hip stabilizers (Glute Medius) to work intensely to maintain pelvic alignment while crossing the midline.
Curtsy Lunge.how_to: 1. Stand tall. 2. Step one foot diagonally behind the other leg as if doing a curtsy. 3. Bend both knees until the front thigh is parallel to the floor. 4. Drive through the front heel to stand back up.
Curtsy Lunge.cue: Push off the front foot to return to start.

Curtsy Lunge.biomechanics: Constant resistance against pure lateral hip abduction, completely removing quadriceps or hamstring assistance.
Curtsy Lunge.how_to: 1. Attach an ankle cuff to a low pulley around your right ankle. 2. Stand with your left side to the machine. 3. Keeping torso upright, use outer hip muscles to pull your right leg directly out to the side.
Curtsy Lunge.cue: Squeeze from the outside of your hips.

Curtsy Lunge.biomechanics: Constant resistance against pure medial hip adduction, targeting the groin musculature directly.
Curtsy Lunge.how_to: 1. Attach cuff to a low pulley around your right ankle. 2. Stand with right side to the machine. 3. Keeping torso stable, engage inner thigh to pull the right leg across the front of your standing leg.
Curtsy Lunge.cue: Drive your inner thighs together forcefully.

Arnold Press.targets: Serratus Anterior (ribs 1-8 to medial border of scapula).
Arnold Press.biomechanics: Unweighted scapular protraction and upward rotation to ensure healthy tracking of the scapula before pressing overhead.
Arnold Press.how_to: 1. Stand facing a wall, arms straight out with palms flat against it. 2. Keep arms straight and let your chest sink toward the wall (scapulae pinch). 3. Push the wall away, rounding your upper back (scapulae spread).
Arnold Press.cue: Push the wall away to hollow out your chest.

Arnold Press.targets: Rotator Cuff (Infraspinatus, Teres Minor) and Trapezius.
Arnold Press.biomechanics: Strict, active upward rotation of the shoulder blades against a fixed vertical plane to improve overhead mobility.
Arnold Press.how_to: 1. Stand with your back, head, and heels flat against a wall. 2. Bring arms up to a 90-degree goal post position against the wall. 3. Slowly slide your hands up the wall into a 'Y', keeping contact. 4. Pull back down.
Arnold Press.cue: Keep lower back flat against the wall.

Arnold Press.targets: Anterior Deltoid (front shoulder), Lateral Deltoid, and Triceps.
Arnold Press.biomechanics: Combines overhead elbow extension with transverse plane shoulder rotation (supination to pronation), heavily taxing the anterior and lateral deltoid heads.
Arnold Press.how_to: 1. Hold dumbbells at shoulder height with palms facing your face (supinated). 2. As you press the weights overhead, rotate your hands so your palms face forward at the top (pronated). 3. Reverse the motion on the way down.
Arnold Press.cue: Uncork your shoulders as you press.

Arnold Press.biomechanics: Leaning away from the pulley alters the resistance curve, shifting the point of maximum tension to the bottom/mid-range of the lateral raise.
Arnold Press.how_to: 1. Stand next to a low cable, holding the machine with your inner hand. 2. Lean your body weight away from the machine. 3. With the outer arm, raise the cable handle straight out to the side. 4. Lower with control.
Arnold Press.cue: Lead with the elbow and push the weight away from you.

Arnold Press.biomechanics: Pure elbow extension isolating the triceps against constant downward cable tension.
Arnold Press.how_to: 1. Face a high cable with a rope or straight bar. 2. Tuck your elbows tightly against your ribs. 3. Push the attachment straight down toward the floor until arms are fully extended. 4. Control the return to 90 degrees.
Arnold Press.cue: Imagine your elbows are nailed to your ribs.

RDL.biomechanics: Unloaded hip hinge to actively groove the exact posterior chain mechanics required for heavy deadlifts.
RDL.how_to: 1. Stand with hands gently behind your head. 2. Keeping a slight bend in your knees and a perfectly flat back, push your hips as far back as possible. 3. Squeeze glutes to stand back up.
RDL.cue: Push your butt to the wall behind you.

RDL.biomechanics: Dynamic stretching of the hamstring insertion at the ischial tuberosity to safely prepare for heavy eccentric loading.
RDL.how_to: 1. Stand sideways to a wall for balance. 2. Keep the working leg relatively straight and swing it forward and backward. 3. Gradually increase the height of the swing.
RDL.cue: Feel the stretch at the end of each swing.

RDL.targets: Hamstrings (Primary), Gluteus Maximus (Primary), and Erector Spinae (Secondary).
RDL.biomechanics: A heavy, compound hip hinge where the knees remain fixed in a slightly bent position, heavily loading the hamstrings during the eccentric phase.
RDL.how_to: 1. Hold a barbell or heavy dumbbells with a shoulder-width stance. 2. Keeping legs nearly straight, push your hips straight back. 3. Lower the weight down the front of your legs until you feel a deep hamstring stretch. 4. Drive hips forward to stand.
RDL.cue: Scrape your shins with the bar.

RDL.biomechanics: The seated position puts the hips in flexion, pre-stretching the hamstrings and isolating pure knee flexion.
RDL.how_to: 1. Sit in the machine with the pad resting just above your Achilles tendons. 2. Lock the thigh pad down tight. 3. Curl the weight down and back as far as possible. 4. Control the weight on the way up.
RDL.cue: Squeeze the hamstring to move the weight, controlling the release.

RDL.targets: Gastrocnemius (femoral condyles to calcaneus via Achilles tendon).
RDL.biomechanics: Keeping the knee fully extended (straight) perfectly isolates and optimally loads the biarticular gastrocnemius muscle.
RDL.how_to: 1. Stand on one foot with the ball of the foot on the edge of a block. 2. Keep the knee locked straight. 3. Drop the heel down for a deep stretch. 4. Press explosively through the ball of the foot to lift the heel high. 5. Lower slowly.
RDL.cue: Drive your body straight up to the ceiling.

Lat Pulldown.biomechanics: Pure, active scapular depression without elbow flexion to neurologically prime the lats before initiating the full pull.
Lat Pulldown.how_to: 1. Grab a pulldown bar with a wide grip and sit down. 2. Keeping your elbows perfectly locked and straight, shrug your shoulders up to your ears. 3. Pull the shoulder blades hard down and together to move the bar just a few inches.
Lat Pulldown.cue: Put your shoulder blades in your back pockets.

Lat Pulldown.biomechanics: Passive vertical suspension to stretch the lats to their full anatomical length and safely decompress the thoracic/lumbar spine.
Lat Pulldown.how_to: 1. Grab a pull-up bar with an overhand grip. 2. Let your feet hang free off the floor. 3. Completely relax your shoulders and back, letting gravity pull your body downward. Hold for 15-30 seconds.
Lat Pulldown.cue: Let gravity pull your hips down and stretch your lats.

Lat Pulldown.targets: Latissimus Dorsi (Primary), Brachialis, and Biceps Brachii.
Lat Pulldown.biomechanics: A vertical pulling motion combining shoulder adduction and elbow flexion to heavily target the width of the back.
Lat Pulldown.how_to: 1. Take a wide grip on the pulldown bar and sit down, locking knees under the pads. 2. Lean back slightly. 3. Pull the bar smoothly down to your upper collarbone. 4. Control the bar back up until arms are fully extended.
Lat Pulldown.cue: Drive your elbows down towards the floor.

Lat Pulldown.targets: Latissimus Dorsi (thoracolumbar fascia to bicipital groove).
Lat Pulldown.biomechanics: Pure shoulder extension against constant tension, entirely removing the biceps from the movement.
Lat Pulldown.how_to: 1. Stand facing a high cable with a straight bar. 2. Grab the bar with straight arms, hinge slightly forward. 3. Push the bar in an arc down to your thighs using only your lats. 4. Resist the weight on the way up.

Lat Pulldown.targets: Brachialis (distal half of anterior humerus to ulnar tuberosity) and Brachioradialis.
Lat Pulldown.biomechanics: Utilizing a neutral (hammer) grip functionally disadvantages the biceps brachii, forcing the underlying brachialis to take the load during elbow flexion.
Lat Pulldown.how_to: 1. Attach a D-handle to a low cable. 2. Stand facing the machine, holding the handle with a neutral grip (palm facing in). 3. Keeping elbow glued to your ribs, curl the handle up. 4. Lower with control.
Lat Pulldown.cue: Keep your wrist locked in a neutral hammer position.
